speaker: interviewer
text: With Peter Fischl. And I'm the interviewer, Trish [? DeGenera ?]. Peter, we were still at the-- when the food--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I want to make a point as to-- to-- with the story that-- that would explain the food problem and the water problem. So-- because the water pipes were bombed away and so on, there was no water, so to speak. We melted snow, and we also kept water in the bathtub. And from the bathtub, we boiled water. And then later on it was cooling off, and we were drinking it as drinking water or cooked with it. So that was our water. The food was that we were able to exchange for various of things, of jewelry or money, whatever, to get some flour and some potatoes and a little fat or something and a little yeast. So my mother was able to hand-make bread and then bake it. So mostly we survived on, as you say, bread and water and on various things. It was very cold weather, and the Germans had horses to-- to pull the heavy artillery on the streets. And naturally then the airplanes came and threw the bombs and killed the horses. So we looked out on the street, and here were a frozen, dead horses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So somebody would go out with a saw and saw the leg off of the horse and part of the leg and then would bring it in and then, as you call it, defrost that leg and they would cook that horse leg, and we would eat it. And it was like a heavenly meal. And so I can tell you that horse meat is a little sweet, but it was fantastic.

speaker: interviewee
text: And with how hungry we were, one time we found some canned food. I don't remember what it was, but then we opened it, and we ate it, and we felt like in heaven, like you would go to the best Hungarian or American restaurant in America and have a $500 dinner. OK, that's how I felt opening and eating that canned food. Now, how that canned food tasted, really, I found out that a couple of years later after the war was over and we already ate normally, I found that canned food, bought it, was thrilled. I was thrilled to death to find this heavenly food. And when I opened it and tasted it, I spitted it out. It was horrible. But that's how hungry we were. And I think these two things probably will explain it to you as how hungry we were and-- and by method of luck of getting foods and stuff like that. And very rarely, a bakery would be open. And we would be going out and standing in line at the bakery, a long line, for bread. And then low-flying aircraft would come and shoot at us with machine guns while we were standing at the bread. But we were so hungry that nobody walked away. The airplanes went away, we got up, and whoever died from a machine-gun bullet was dead, and the rest of us waited for our turn to get our bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody was hiding, right? They were waiting for the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: They weren't necessarily Jews, no. They were all kind of people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you and your family-- and your mother, your sister, and you, and your father was not here, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were still in hiding in this apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I forgot to-- to mention something, which is very dramatic. I was still in this Catholic school. That was November 27, 1944, that my father called me in this Catholic school. And he told me that the German troops surrounded the ambulance station where he was standing, and he'd be taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where to? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he said, taken-- we're going to be taken away, probably to Germany or something. But I don't remember if he said that. I don't recall that. But he just said 'be taken away' and I think it meant to us a death camp kind of thing. And so I spoke with him at age 14. I spoke with my father for five minutes before he was taken away. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [SOB] I wish that there going to be no kid in this universe who will have to experience anything like that ever-- ever. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] going back where we lived, close to the end of the war we were liberated there. We were liberated there by the Russians. And-- and then what we heard is that my father is alive. He's on his way home. And he never came home. He never came home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] for many years, quite frequently I would have dreams that-- that my father arrived home. And I would be crying. And I would say, Father, that is fantastic you are home. And then the next time when I would have this dream, I would talk to him about the dream I had before that he came home. And I said, isn't it interesting that I had a dream that you came home?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was an endless, endless motion picture, so to speak, where I had these dreams after dreams. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] probably-- probably all this and more what I saw, which is just a little part I was telling, because I saw many things during World War II and experienced things, which I have no immediate recollection. But it's in me, is that I have this tremendous head to head against prejudicedness and that-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I don't know if I ever can forgive. No, honestly, I don't think I can ever forgive, probably, which is not to right. But somebody said, they said, since I cannot forget, I cannot forgive. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I'm thinking of my 6 million brothers and sisters who were so ruthlessly treated and executed. So therefore, this is my feeling about this matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your father was coming from, back? He was coming back from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Mauthausen, if he did. This thing is foggy. Some people said that he started coming home from Mauthausen. But many people were practically dead when they were liberated. So coming home, you know, I don't know what it-- what it really meant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Russians liberated you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your mother. Can you describe what happened, I mean, how it happened? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. The night before, there were some German soldiers coming down. And that time, we were already in the bunker because the situation was such that the Russians were already at end of the street. So we weren't really afraid that anybody going to call the Germans or the Gestapo. Everybody was afraid for his own life. And so some German soldiers came down to the bunker. And they were not Nazis, so to speak. They were Wehrmacht soldiers, just foot soldiers, not SS or anything like that. And so at that time, I spoke fluent German. It's an interesting thing that as a kid I spoke fluent German. I spoke German better than Hungarian. But today I don't speak German. My mind entirely threw the German language out of my mind. I-- I don't even want to hear a German word. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Wehrmacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Wehrmacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Wehrmacht came in. And then they told us that the war is over the Russians just completely encircled Budapest. And that is-- that's it. And I pulled my sister apart. And they were very tired, and they fell asleep there. I think it was three or four of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they felt asleep there. And then I talked with my sister that we didn't know how to operate the rifles, but couldn't we shoot them somehow? We were thinking. We definitely had this idea of shooting them. But we actually gave up because we didn't know even how to load the rifle or how to shoot with it and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the Germans left very early in the morning. And as they walked out, in the next hour we had the first Russian soldiers coming down. And we were liberated right there in that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's a bunker? I mean, like other people were staying with you, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bunker means-- is usually a basement of a house or an apartment building, which is usually not made for a bunker. A bunker in the reality would be something made out of very strong materials, steel and cement, underground in order to save you from heavy bombings. And then you would go down with stairs, winding stairs, 100 feet down. And you would be in the cement bunker. So if there are some direct hit with bombs, they wouldn't kill you. But the bunker in our meaning was the basement of any house, so where we usually during the winter time we would keep burning wood for our stoves, a storage place under the house. So when I am referring to a bunker, that was the bunker I am referring to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you the only family there, or other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole house, the whole apartment building was living under the bunker-- everybody-- I don't know, maybe 50 people or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Russians came in, what happened? Everybody-- threw everybody-- everybody went out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of the people lived there in this-- we were there only, you know, people who came during the war there. And then people looked at their eyes, and they said, you are Jews, aren't you? And we said, yes, we are. And they were just really-- they know it all the time along that we were, but they wouldn't report us to the Gestapo or anything. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's what happened, basically. And then we-- we moved back to-- to the original place where we lived. And then my father never came back. And then within one year, communism took over, very hard communism took over. So you went back to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I went back to school a few years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] because for me, communism was so depressing and just dictatorship, that I took a job as a truck driver because of the freedom involved being-- driving a truck. So I-- I drove the truck, and while they were loading my truck, I wrote short stories. And at age 18, many of them-- I would write 40, 50 pages a day. And, as a matter of fact, during-- during the whole Holocaust, it was one of my strong things in my life that I had a very strong imagination. And I was capable of making up stories in my life and entertain myself and better my life by making fun and false images in my head when I wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you trucked for how long? I mean, how did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was living through communism for about 10 years, where I was trucking there a few years. Most of the time I was a truck driver. And yes, yes, yes. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in 1956, we had the Hungarian Revolution started against the Russians. And it broke out, and the Russians tricked us in and killed the whole new government of-- the free government of Hungary it was executed. And the Russians came back. And all-- all-- all transportation was gone. There was no transportation. Everybody was on strike or afraid to work. And so I decided this is the last straw. If I live or die, I'm leaving. But there was no transportation. So I talked to a friend of mine and I said, tomorrow night-- and that was the first time I really was a leader. I told him, we break in to the garage where I work, and we gonna take my truck out and go for the border, and we gonna escape. And that's what we did. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '56. 1956. And we stole my truck out before the papers in the office there by using coins and putting them-- putting ink on the coins and then putting that on the top of the paper and smearing them so it won't read exactly. So it looked like a stamp, an official stamp. And we made some phony signatures that we gonna go with it. Because in a dictatorship, you need a paper for everything. So we made papers for ourselves that we going to some border towns to pick up some food. So-- and at the meantime, all my friends wanted to escape. So I took 32 people with me on my truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents, too? I mean your mom and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mom and my-- no. My mom and my sister didn't want to come. And that's a very long story. But during communism these 10 years, several times I wanted to escape. And nobody wanted to join me. And at this time when the revolution was over in '56. I had it. And so I said, OK, you are afraid. I'm going. And this Free Europe radio, I'm going to give the signal if they killed me or not. And if they didn't kill me, I hope you are coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: So with my truck and these 32 people, we made it to the border. We got arrested the first attempt. We walked 15 miles, and we got arrested. And we spent one night in jail. But so many thousands of people were-- were trying to escape and so many were arrested, there was no room in the jails. So if they-- they interrogated each of us, and they questioned us who we are. And if they didn't think that we are the people who are going to go out and shoot them and get-- then they let us go. And they put us-- our name on a list. And they said, Peter Fischl, you can go home to Budapest, but if we-- if we catch you one more time, you are on this list, you're going to Siberia, which is a cold place in Russia, and you're going to spend the rest of your life there. So I went out to the train station. And this is again something interesting. I think it is. Because there was about, I don't know, 5,000 people, dirty, unshaven, you know, looked like all homeless-- we would be homeless people. Except there was one couple who looked like they just stepped out from Fifth Avenue store, beautifully dressed, combed, hat and everything. So I-- my mind told me, these people, this is the first time they want to escape. These people are out of their mind. So I was always concerned about other people in my life. So I got up, and I walked to them real far away, and I asked their permission, can I sit down? And they said yes. So I told them what happened to me last night, that we got arrested, we got shot at with machine guns on the border-- I didn't tell you that-- when they stopped us. And so I'm telling this story to these people to talk them out of this escape effort. Because I said, you can't leave. So this boy says, what are you telling me? He said, I just came back from Austria, and this is my girlfriend. And I went back to Budapest, and we are here. And tomorrow morning, we're going to Austria again. I said-- I was shocked. So I said, that's the kind of connections you have? He says, yes. I said, could we join you? And because he saw how much I was concerned about his life, he let me go with him. And he had sensational contacts at the border. And that's how for the second time we made it. And that's how I-- I got to Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when I got to Austria, I went to the Free Europe station, radio station, and gave a coded signal to my sister and mother that I'm here and come. So my sister came, but my mother was too cowardly. My mother always felt that we're never going to make anything, you know. And I just couldn't live with that anymore. And she could have made it. And they couldn't give up things. And I gave everything up in Hungary. I still was semi-wealthy. I had, you know-- I had my Persian rugs and chandeliers and beautiful furniture and stuff like that. But I turned my head, and I walked on, and I never looked back. I had it. I had enough. And when I crossed the border finally in Austria, I went down on my knees, and I kissed the ground. I was so happy. Then in Austria, I went to the American embassy and applied for an American visa. And then I got my visa. And there was an American ship called which was an American transportation ship for the Navy, an enormous, big ship. 5,000 people got on it. And that's how I came to America, to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me-- the people that you were with crossing the border in Austria-- did you ever find out who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the people I knew because they were immediate friends. Alexander was one who came with me, the man who saved my sister's life. And by that time, he was not married to my sister anymore. And many people from the apartment building where I lived came. And then the word spread around that Peter is leaving. Then it didn't take too long that my truck filled up. And many people I didn't know before.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said those two people you met at the train station-- who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the two people. The two people after-- that's also interesting. Because after we went to Austria, we parted, and I never met them in my life anymore. I kissed them. I hugged them. And I never met them anymore. I don't know who they were. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is also something I might call strange happening in life, where you get the strange feeling that, what really happened here in your life? That's my feeling about these people, that-- One-- I would like to ask you what happened to you your-- the properties and things that your father had when the communists--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans and-- and-- and the Russians took-- took everything. What the Germans couldn't take, the Russians took. For instance, they took over a 37-unit apartment building which belonged to my sister and me in a very beautiful, number-one area with storefronts and a very wealthy area. And just recently, the Hungarian government decided that they-- they're going to compensate us, and they paid us $7,000 compensation.
